title: Bottom view of tree
description: Print the nodes visible from the bottom view of a binary tree

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7
examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize queue=[(1,0)], dict={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): dict[0]=1, enqueue (2,-1) and (3,1). Queue=[(2,-1),(3,1)]. Step 3: Process (2,-1): dict[-1]=2, enqueue (4,-2) and (5,0). Queue=[(3,1),(4,-2),(5,0)]. Step 4: Process (3,1): dict[1]=3, enqueue (6,0) and (7,2). Queue=[(4,-2),(5,0),(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 5: Process (4,-2): dict[-2]=4, no children. Queue=[(5,0),(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 6: Process (5,0): dict[0]=5 (update), no children. Queue=[(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 7: Process (6,0): dict[0]=6 (update), no children. Queue=[(7,2)]. Step 8: Process (7,2): dict[2]=7, no children. Queue empty. Step 9: Sort dict by HD: -2:4, -1:2, 0:6, 1:3, 2:7. Output [4,2,6,3,7].

examples.input: Tree: 20 (root), left: 8, right: 22; 8 has left: 5, right: 3; 22 has right: 25; 3 has left: 10, right: 14

approaches.name: Approach 1: Level Order Traversal with Horizontal Distance
approaches.summary: Perform a level-order traversal (BFS) of the tree, tracking each node's horizontal distance from the root. For each horizontal distance, update a map with the last node seen at that distance, as bottom view requires the deepest node. Use a queue to process nodes level by level.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bottom View of Binary Tree using Level Order Traversal with Horizontal Distance
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to print nodes visible from the bottom view of a binary tree
ladders.explanation: The bottom view of a binary tree shows the nodes that would be visible if you looked at the tree from below. For each vertical line (horizontal distance from root), we need the deepest node along that line. This approach uses BFS (level order traversal) to process nodes level by level, ensuring we get the deepest nodes. We track each node's horizontal distance (HD) where root has HD=0, left child has HD=parent_HD-1, and right child has HD=parent_HD+1. We maintain a dictionary that maps HD to the node's value, updating it whenever we encounter a node at that HD (since BFS processes from top to bottom, the last update for each HD will be the deepest node). The algorithm: 1. Initialize queue with root (HD=0) and dictionary. 2. While queue not empty: dequeue node, update dictionary[HD]=node.value, enqueue left child with HD-1, enqueue right child with HD+1. 3. After traversal, sort dictionary by HD and return values. Time: O(n) for traversal + O(w log w) for sorting where w is width (number of unique HDs), but w≤n so O(n log n) worst case. Space: O(n) for queue and dictionary. Connection to L1: This uses the level order traversal with HD tracking as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling null nodes, forgetting to sort by HD, using DFS instead of BFS (which wouldn't guarantee deepest nodes). Real code pattern: Use collections.deque for queue, dictionary for HD mapping.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue=[(1,0)], dict={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): dict[0]=1, enqueue (2,-1) and (3,1). Queue=[(2,-1),(3,1)]. Step 3: Process (2,-1): dict[-1]=2, enqueue (4,-2) and (5,0). Queue=[(3,1),(4,-2),(5,0)]. Step 4: Process (3,1): dict[1]=3, enqueue (6,0) and (7,2). Queue=[(4,-2),(5,0),(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 5: Process (4,-2): dict[-2]=4, no children. Queue=[(5,0),(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 6: Process (5,0): dict[0]=5 (update), no children. Queue=[(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 7: Process (6,0): dict[0]=6 (update), no children. Queue=[(7,2)]. Step 8: Process (7,2): dict[2]=7, no children. Queue empty. Step 9: Sort dict by HD: -2:4, -1:2, 0:6, 1:3, 2:7. Output [4,2,6,3,7].

examples.input: Tree: 20 (root), left: 8, right: 22; 8 has left: 5, right: 3; 22 has right: 25; 3 has left: 10, right: 14

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has right: 4; 3 has left: 5
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7
Tree: 20, left: 8, right: 22; 8 has left: 5, right: 3; 22 has right: 25; 3 has left: 10, right: 14
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 4 has left: 6
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4; 3 has right: 5; 4 has right: 6; 5 has left: 7

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Level Order Traversal with Horizontal Distance Tracking
ladders.desc: Perform BFS on binary tree while tracking horizontal distance of each node
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core engine for the bottom view problem. It traverses the tree level by level using a queue (BFS), and for each node, it computes and stores its horizontal distance (HD). HD represents the vertical column position: root=0, move left subtract 1, move right add 1. The intuition: BFS ensures we process nodes from top to bottom, so by tracking HD, we can identify which vertical column each node belongs to. This is crucial because bottom view needs the deepest node in each column. Algorithm: 1. Initialize queue with (root, HD=0). 2. While queue not empty: dequeue (node, hd). 3. Process node (e.g., store hd in a list for debugging). 4. If node.left exists, enqueue (node.left, hd-1). 5. If node.right exists, enqueue (node.right, hd+1). Time: O(n) as each node visited once. Space: O(n) for queue in worst case (complete tree). Connection to L0: This directly provides the traversal and HD data needed to determine bottom view nodes. Common mistakes: Forgetting to pass HD to children, not handling null root, using stack instead of queue (would be DFS). Real code pattern: Use deque for efficient queue operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[(1,0)]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,0), process, enqueue (2,-1) and (3,1). Queue=[(2,-1),(3,1)]. Step 3: Dequeue (2,-1), process, no children. Queue=[(3,1)]. Step 4: Dequeue (3,1), process, no children. Queue empty. Output list of processed nodes: [(1,0), (2,-1), (3,1)].

Step 1: queue=[(1,0)]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,0), process, enqueue (2,-1),(3,1). Queue=[(2,-1),(3,1)]. Step 3: Dequeue (2,-1), process, enqueue (4,-2). Queue=[(3,1),(4,-2)]. Step 4: Dequeue (3,1), process, no children. Queue=[(4,-2)]. Step 5: Dequeue (4,-2), process, no children. Queue empty. Output: [(1,0), (2,-1), (3,1), (4,-2)].

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4; 3 has right: 7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Updating Last Node for Each Horizontal Distance
ladders.desc: Maintain a map where key=horizontal distance, value=last node value seen at that distance
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight for bottom view: for each horizontal distance (vertical column), we want the deepest node. Since we traverse level by level (BFS), the last node we encounter at a given HD will be the deepest one in that column. We maintain a dictionary (or map) where HD is the key and node value is the value. Each time we process a node with a certain HD, we update dictionary[hd] = node.value. This overwrites any previous value for that HD, ensuring only the last (deepest) node is kept. Intuition: Imagine drawing vertical lines through the tree at each HD; BFS paints nodes from top to bottom, so the final paint on each line is the bottom-most node. Algorithm: 1. Initialize empty dict. 2. During traversal, for each (node, hd): dict[hd] = node.value. 3. After traversal, dict contains bottom view nodes. Time: O(1) per update, O(n) total. Space: O(w) where w is number of unique HDs (width of tree). Connection to L1: This logic uses the HD from the traversal to update the map. Common mistakes: Using first node instead of last (would give top view), not updating when same HD appears again. Real code pattern: Python dict with simple assignment.

examples.trace: Step 1: dict={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): dict[0]=1. Step 3: Process (2,-1): dict[-1]=2. Step 4: Process (3,1): dict[1]=3. Step 5: Process (4,-2): dict[-2]=4. Step 6: Process (5,0): dict[0]=5 (overwrites 1). Final dict: {-2:4, -1:2, 0:5, 1:3}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Horizontal Distance Concept in Binary Trees
ladders.desc: Assign horizontal distance values to nodes based on their position relative to root
ladders.explanation: Horizontal distance (HD) is a coordinate system for binary trees where each node gets an integer representing its vertical column. Root has HD=0. For any node, its left child has HD = parent_HD - 1, and right child has HD = parent_HD + 1. Intuition: Imagine drawing the tree with root at center; moving left decreases HD (negative values), moving right increases HD (positive values). Nodes with same HD are vertically aligned. This concept is fundamental for problems involving vertical order, top/bottom view, and diagonal traversal. Algorithm to compute HD for all nodes: 1. Start at root with HD=0. 2. Recursively or iteratively assign HD to children using the rules. Time: O(n) to visit all nodes. Space: O(n) to store HDs if needed. Connection to L2: HD provides the keys for the map in bottom view logic. Common mistakes: Confusing HD with depth (level), not handling negative HDs correctly. Real code pattern: Store HD as an integer property or pass as parameter during traversal.

examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1 has HD=0. Step 2: Left child 2: HD = 0-1 = -1. Step 3: Right child 3: HD = 0+1 = 1. Output: 1:0, 2:-1, 3:1.
Step 1: Root 1: HD=0. Step 2: Left child 2: HD=0-1=-1. Step 3: Right child 3: HD=0+1=1. Step 4: Left child of 2 (node 4): HD = -1-1 = -2. Output: 1:0, 2:-1, 3:1, 4:-2.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4; 3 has right: 7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Level Order Traversal (BFS) of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Visit all nodes of a binary tree level by level using a queue
ladders.explanation: Level order traversal, also known as Breadth-First Search (BFS), processes nodes row by row starting from root. It uses a queue data structure: enqueue root, then repeatedly dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its children. Intuition: This ensures we visit all nodes at depth d before moving to depth d+1, mimicking how you'd read the tree from top to bottom. It's essential for problems where depth or level matters. Algorithm: 1. If root is null, return. 2. Initialize queue with root. 3. While queue not empty: dequeue node, process node.value, if node.left exists enqueue it, if node.right exists enqueue it. Time: O(n) as each node visited once. Space: O(n) for queue in worst case (complete tree). Connection to L3: BFS provides the order in which we assign HD and update the map. Common mistakes: Using list as queue (pop(0) is O(n)), forgetting to check for null children. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque in Python for O(1) enqueue/dequeue.

examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process 1, enqueue 2 and 3. Queue=[2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process 2, no children. Queue=[3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process 3, no children. Queue empty. Output order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: queue=[1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process 1, enqueue 2,3. Queue=[2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process 2, enqueue 4. Queue=[3,4]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process 3, no children. Queue=[4]. Step 5: Dequeue 4, process 4, no children. Queue empty. Output: 1,2,3,4.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4; 3 has right: 7

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree node representation and the idea of visiting nodes
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically stores a value and references to its children. In code, a node is often defined as a class with attributes: value, left, right. Traversal means visiting each node exactly once in some order. Basic traversals include pre-order (root, left, right), in-order (left, root, right), post-order (left, right, root), and level-order (by levels). Intuition: Trees are like family trees or organizational charts; traversal is how you 'walk' through all members. This foundation is crucial for any tree algorithm. Example node class in Python: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Time for any traversal: O(n) since we visit n nodes. Space: O(h) for recursive traversals where h is height, or O(n) for BFS. Connection to L4: Level order traversal builds on this by using a queue to visit nodes level by level. Common mistakes: Not handling null (None) children, infinite recursion if cycles exist. Real code pattern: Define Node class and use recursion or iteration to traverse.

examples.input: Tree with nodes: 1 (root), left child 2, right child 3
examples.output: Node structure: Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Create Node(1). Step 2: Create Node(2) and set as left child of Node(1). Step 3: Create Node(3) and set as right child of Node(1). Result: Node(1) has left pointing to Node(2), right pointing to Node(3). Node(2) and Node(3) have left=None, right=None.

examples.output: Node structure: Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); Node(2) has left=Node(4)
examples.trace: Step 1: Create Node(1). Step 2: Create Node(2), set as left of Node(1). Step 3: Create Node(3), set as right of Node(1). Step 4: Create Node(4), set as left of Node(2). Result: Node(1): left->Node(2), right->Node(3). Node(2): left->Node(4), right=None. Node(3): left=None, right=None. Node(4): left=None, right=None.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5
testCases.expected: Node(1): left->Node(2), right->Node(3); Node(2): left->Node(4), right->Node(5)

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4; 3 has right: 7
testCases.expected: Node(1): left->Node(2), right->Node(3); Node(2): left->Node(4); Node(3): right->Node(7)

approaches.name: Approach 3: Vertical Order Traversal and Take Last
approaches.summary: First compute the vertical order traversal of the tree by grouping nodes by horizontal distance. For each vertical group, select the last node in that group (based on level order within the group) to form the bottom view. This separates the problem into two clear steps.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bottom View of Binary Tree Using Vertical Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution that computes vertical order traversal and selects last node from each vertical group.
ladders.explanation: The bottom view of a binary tree shows nodes that are visible when looking from below, where nodes with the same horizontal distance (HD) from root are overlapped, and the one with the highest level (closest to bottom) wins. This approach solves it in two clear steps: first compute vertical order traversal by grouping nodes by HD, then for each vertical group, select the last node when traversed in level order (BFS) to get the deepest node. Intuition: Imagine projecting the tree onto a vertical line; nodes with same HD align vertically, and the bottom-most node in each column is visible. Step-by-step: 1. Perform BFS traversal starting from root (HD=0). 2. Use a map (e.g., dictionary) to store lists of (node value, level) keyed by HD. 3. For each node, compute HD: left child gets HD-1, right child gets HD+1. 4. Append (node.val, level) to the list for that HD. 5. After BFS, for each HD in sorted order, take the last element from its list (since BFS processes level by level, last element has highest level). 6. Collect node values in order of HD. Time: O(n log n) due to sorting HDs; BFS is O(n). Space: O(n) for queue and map. Connection to L1: This uses the vertical order traversal helper as a key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling ties correctly (must take last node in level order), forgetting to sort HDs, or incorrect HD calculation. Real code pattern: Use deque for BFS, defaultdict(list) for map, sort keys at end.

examples.trace: Step 1: BFS with HD. Queue: [(1,0,0)] where (node, HD, level). Map starts empty. Step 2: Process (1,0,0): map[0] = [(1,0)]. Enqueue left (2,-1,1) and right (3,1,1). Step 3: Process (2,-1,1): map[-1] = [(2,1)]. Enqueue left (4,-2,2) and right (5,0,2). Step 4: Process (3,1,1): map[1] = [(3,1)]. Enqueue left (6,0,2) and right (7,2,2). Step 5: Process (4,-2,2): map[-2] = [(4,2)]. No children. Step 6: Process (5,0,2): map[0] = [(1,0), (5,2)]. Step 7: Process (6,0,2): map[0] = [(1,0), (5,2), (6,2)]. Step 8: Process (7,2,2): map[2] = [(7,2)]. Step 9: Sort HDs: [-2,-1,0,1,2]. For each: take last node. HD=-2: last (4,2) -> 4. HD=-1: last (2,1) -> 2. HD=0: last (6,2) -> 6. HD=1: last (3,1) -> 3. HD=2: last (7,2) -> 7. Output list: [4,2,6,3,7].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Vertical Order Traversal of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Compute groups of nodes by horizontal distance, storing lists of (value, level).
ladders.explanation: This subroutine groups nodes based on their horizontal distance (HD) from the root, which is essential for solving the bottom view problem. Intuition: HD represents how far left or right a node is; nodes with same HD form a vertical column. By collecting them in level order (BFS), we preserve depth information. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize queue with (root, HD=0, level=0). 2. Use a map (dictionary) where key=HD, value=list of (node.value, level). 3. While queue not empty: dequeue (node, hd, level). Append (node.val, level) to map[hd]. 4. If node has left child, enqueue (left, hd-1, level+1). 5. If node has right child, enqueue (right, hd+1, level+1). 6. After BFS, return map. Time: O(n) for BFS traversal. Space: O(n) for queue and map. Connection to L0: This map provides the vertical groups; L0 then processes it to get bottom view. Common mistakes: Using DFS instead of BFS (loses level order), not tracking level, or incorrect HD updates. Real code pattern: deque for queue, defaultdict(list) for map.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [(1,0,0)]. Map empty. Step 2: Process (1,0,0): map[0]=[(1,0)]. Enqueue left (2,-1,1) and right (3,1,1). Step 3: Process (2,-1,1): map[-1]=[(2,1)]. No children. Step 4: Process (3,1,1): map[1]=[(3,1)]. No children. Final map: {-1: [(2,1)], 0: [(1,0)], 1: [(3,1)]}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Grouping Nodes by Horizontal Distance in BFS
ladders.desc: Traverse tree level-by-level while tracking horizontal distance to form vertical columns.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves performing a breadth-first search (BFS) on the binary tree while assigning a horizontal distance (HD) to each node. HD is computed relative to the root: root has HD=0, moving left decreases HD by 1, moving right increases HD by 1. By traversing level-by-level (BFS), we ensure nodes are processed in order of increasing depth, which is crucial for correctly identifying the bottom-most node later. Intuition: Think of the tree on a grid; HD is the x-coordinate, and level is the y-coordinate. BFS visits nodes row by row (level by level), so when we group by HD, we get lists ordered by level. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize queue with (root, HD=0). 2. While queue not empty: dequeue (node, hd). 3. Record node in a data structure keyed by hd (e.g., append to list for that hd). 4. If left child exists, enqueue (left, hd-1). 5. If right child exists, enqueue (right, hd+1). Time: O(n) as each node visited once. Space: O(n) for queue. Connection to L1: This is the traversal part of vertical order; L1 adds level tracking. Common mistakes: Using DFS (which doesn't preserve level order), not updating HD correctly, or missing queue initialization. Real code pattern: Use deque, loop while queue.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [(A,0)]. Groups empty. Step 2: Process (A,0): groups[0]=[A]. Enqueue left (B,-1) and right (C,1). Step 3: Process (B,-1): groups[-1]=[B]. No children. Step 4: Process (C,1): groups[1]=[C]. No children. Final groups: {-1:[B], 0:[A], 1:[C]}.

Step 1: Queue: [(1,0)]. Step 2: Process (1,0): groups[0]=[1]. Enqueue (2,-1) and (3,1). Step 3: Process (2,-1): groups[-1]=[2]. Enqueue left (4,-2). Step 4: Process (3,1): groups[1]=[3]. Step 5: Process (4,-2): groups[-2]=[4]. Final groups: {-2:[4], -1:[2], 0:[1], 1:[3]}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Breadth-First Search (BFS) on Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary tree level by level using a queue.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a fundamental traversal algorithm that visits nodes in order of their depth from the root. It starts at the root, explores all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next level. Intuition: Imagine water spreading evenly from the root; BFS ensures we visit nodes closer to root first. This is essential for problems requiring level-order processing, like the bottom view where depth matters. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize a queue (e.g., deque) and enqueue the root. 2. While queue is not empty: a. Dequeue a node. b. Process the node (e.g., record its value). c. Enqueue its left child if exists. d. Enqueue its right child if exists. Time: O(n) where n is number of nodes, as each node enqueued and dequeued once. Space: O(n) in worst case (e.g., complete tree) for queue. Connection to L2: BFS provides the level-order traversal; L2 adds HD tracking. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check for null children, using stack instead of queue, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque, while queue: node = queue.popleft().

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process 1, enqueue left 2 and right 3. Queue: [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process 2, no children. Queue: [3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process 3, no children. Queue empty. Order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: Queue: [A]. Step 2: Dequeue A, process A, enqueue B and C. Queue: [B,C]. Step 3: Dequeue B, process B, enqueue D. Queue: [C,D]. Step 4: Dequeue C, process C, no children. Queue: [D]. Step 5: Dequeue D, process D, no children. Order: A,B,C,D.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Use a queue to manage nodes in first-in-first-out order during BFS.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a data structure that follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order, meaning the first element added is the first removed. In tree traversal, we use a queue to store nodes to be visited, ensuring level-order processing. Intuition: Think of a line at a ticket counter; people join at the back and leave from the front. Similarly, in BFS, we add child nodes to the back of the queue and process nodes from the front. Basic operations: enqueue (add to back), dequeue (remove from front), and check if empty. Step-by-step for BFS: 1. Enqueue root. 2. While queue not empty: a. Dequeue a node. b. Process it. c. Enqueue its children (left then right). Time per operation: O(1) for enqueue/dequeue if using efficient implementation like deque. Space: O(n) for storing nodes. Connection to L3: This queue enables BFS traversal. Common mistakes: Using list for queue with pop(0) (O(n) time), not handling empty queue, or enqueuing null nodes. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append(root); while queue: node = queue.popleft().

examples.input: Start queue: [A], process: enqueue B, C; then dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial queue: [A]. Step 2: Dequeue A (process A). Step 3: Enqueue B and C to back: queue becomes [B, C]. Step 4: Next dequeue will be B.

examples.input: Queue: [X, Y], dequeue X, enqueue Z
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [X, Y]. Step 2: Dequeue X (process X). Queue: [Y]. Step 3: Enqueue Z to back: queue becomes [Y, Z].

testCases.input: Start queue: [A, B], dequeue, enqueue C
Start queue: [X], dequeue, enqueue Y, dequeue
Start queue: [A, B, C], dequeue, dequeue, enqueue D, E
Start queue: [Q], enqueue R, dequeue, enqueue S
Start queue: [alpha], dequeue, enqueue beta, gamma, dequeue, enqueue delta

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree nodes and the idea of visiting nodes in some order.
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically contains a value and references to its children. Traversal means visiting each node exactly once in a specific order. Intuition: Think of a family tree; traversal is like meeting each person in a sequence. For the bottom view problem, we need to visit all nodes to determine visibility. Basic concepts: 1. Node: has val, left, right attributes. 2. Root: top node. 3. Leaf: node with no children. 4. Traversal orders: pre-order (root, left, right), in-order (left, root, right), post-order (left, right, root), level-order (by depth). Step-by-step for simple traversal: Start at root, recursively visit left subtree, then right subtree. Time: O(n) to visit n nodes. Space: O(h) for recursion depth, where h is tree height. Connection to L4: Traversal uses queues or recursion; BFS is a type of level-order traversal. Common mistakes: Confusing tree with other structures, not handling null nodes, or infinite recursion. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root node with value 1. Step 2: Visit left child: node with value 2. Step 3: Visit right child: node with value 3. All nodes visited.
Step 1: Root A. Step 2: Left child B. Step 3: From B, left child C. Step 4: No more children. Values: A, B, C.
